facts.text: Bardonia takes its name from the Bardon brothers—John, Phillip, and Conrad—who arrived from Bavaria in 1849 and opened several local businesses.
The town’s first railroad stop opened in 1875 on land sold by the Bardon brothers, originally called Bardon's Station before adopting the town’s name in 1891.
A 19th‑century distillery and general store founded by John Bardon helped turn the area into a bustling community around the new train station.
By the 1880s, the area—sometimes called a “section called Germany” in contemporary accounts—boasted thriving farms, a church, and several commercial enterprises.
Bardonia lies just north of the New York State Thruway, a major highway that has long shaped local access and viewsheds.
Today, the nearest active train station is 2.5 miles south in Nanuet, as Bardonia’s own station closed in the early 20th century.
